items.question_text: Per 6-1-1, mileage-based (DME and/or ATD) procedures and minima are authorized only when direct pilot/controller _____ or _____ voice communications are maintained.
items.explanation: 6-1-1 requires direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications. The specific frequency band names are the operational prerequisite.

choices.text: VHF
UHF

items.question_text: A controller has solid radar contact identifying both aircraft and functioning DME equipment, but voice communications with the pilot are being relayed through a third aircraft. Under 6-1-1, may the controller apply mileage-based separation between the two aircraft?
items.explanation: 6-1-1 requires direct pilot/controller voice communications. Relayed voice communications do not satisfy the requirement, regardless of radar contact quality or DME availability.

choices.text: Yes, provided the relaying aircraft is also IFR and on the same frequency.
Yes, because radar contact and DME are both operational.
Yes, because any form of voice communication with the pilot satisfies the paragraph.
No, because direct pilot/controller voice communications are not maintained.

items.question_text: A controller thinks that whenever a DME readout is displayed on the scope for an aircraft, mileage-based minima may be applied. Is this understanding correct under 6-1-1?
items.explanation: 6-1-1 ties authorization to the communications method, not to DME equipment status. DME display alone is insufficient if direct voice communications are not maintained with the pilot.

choices.text: Yes; DME display is the only condition the paragraph specifies.
No; mileage-based minima require direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications.
Yes, provided the DME readout is from a certified ground station.
No; the paragraph requires both DME and ATD for mileage-based minima.

items.question_text: Per 6-1-2, when a position report affecting separation is not received, take action to obtain the report no later than _____ minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.
items.explanation: 6-1-2 specifies exactly 5 minutes as the deadline for obtaining a position report affecting separation. This is a precise threshold that must be memorized.

items.question_text: An aircraft misses a routine position report that does not affect separation with any other known traffic. A second position report that would affect separation is also not received. Under 6-1-2, which report triggers the mandatory action?
items.explanation: 6-1-2 applies explicitly when a position report affecting separation is not received. The paragraph does not create an obligation for reports that do not affect separation.

choices.text: Both reports trigger mandatory action because any missed position report must be obtained.
Only the report affecting separation triggers mandatory action.
Neither report triggers action until two consecutive reports are missed.
Only the first missed report matters because once a report is missed the rule is suspended.

items.question_text: A controller decides to wait until the pilot misses a second position report before taking any action on the first missed report that affects separation. Under 6-1-2, is this the correct procedure?
items.explanation: 6-1-2 requires action no later than 5 minutes after the estimate over the fix. Waiting for a second missed report would likely exceed that deadline and violate the requirement.

choices.text: No; the controller must wait exactly 5 minutes, then may take action or not at their discretion.
Yes; controllers may wait for two consecutive missed reports to conserve frequency time.
Yes, provided the controller issues a traffic advisory in the meantime.
No; action must be taken no later than 5 minutes after the aircraft was estimated over the fix.

items.question_text: An aircraft is being handed off from an en route facility to a terminal facility. The receiving facility requests that the aircraft make the same position report that the transferring facility already required. Under 6-1-3, may the receiving facility require that duplicate report?
items.explanation: 6-1-3 prohibits requiring an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility. A handoff does not create an exception; the transferring and receiving facilities are different facilities.

choices.text: Yes, provided the receiving facility was not monitoring the transferring facility's frequency.
Yes; handoff situations are exempt from the duplicate-report restriction.
No; the same position report must not be required to more than one facility.
No, unless the aircraft is crossing an ARTCC boundary and the report is for a compulsory fix.

items.question_text: A controller reasons that 6-1-3 only applies when both facilities are towers, not when one is an en route center. Is this distinction valid?
items.explanation: 6-1-3 uses the general term 'facility' without any qualification about facility type. The prohibition applies regardless of whether the facilities are towers, centers, or any combination.

choices.text: Yes; the section is titled for terminal operations and does not apply to en route centers.
No; 6-1-3 applies to all facilities without distinguishing by facility type.
Yes; duplicate position reports are only restricted for tower-to-tower coordination.
No, but it applies only when both facilities use the same radio frequency.

items.question_text: Per 6-1-4a, in terminal adjacent-airport operations, Category E, F, G, H, or I aircraft behind a Category C aircraft require _____ minutes of wake turbulence separation.
items.explanation: 6-1-4a3 specifies 2 minutes for Category E, F, G, H, or I behind Category C in terminal adjacent-airport operations.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-4a terminal adjacent-airport minima, which of the following correctly states the wake turbulence separation for Category I behind Category E?
items.explanation: 6-1-4a4 specifies 2 minutes for Category I aircraft behind Category E in terminal adjacent-airport operations.

choices.text: 3 minutes.
2 minutes.
4 minutes.
No wake turbulence separation is required for this category pair.

items.question_text: In en route adjacent-airport operations under 6-1-4b, a heavy aircraft following a super aircraft requires _____ minutes, while a heavy aircraft following a heavy aircraft requires _____ minutes.
items.explanation: 6-1-4b1 gives 3 minutes behind super; 6-1-4b2 gives 2 minutes behind heavy. Distinguishing these two is essential for correct en route adjacent-airport separation.

items.question_text: In terminal adjacent-airport operations under 6-1-4a, which of the following category combinations requires 3 minutes of wake turbulence separation?
items.explanation: 6-1-4a1 specifies 3 minutes for ANY Category B through I aircraft behind Category A. The other combinations use 2 minutes according to 6-1-4a2 through a4.

choices.text: Category B, C, D, E, F, G, H, or I behind Category B or D.
Category B, C, D, E, F, G, H, or I behind Category A.
Category E, F, G, H, or I behind Category C.
Category I behind Category E.

items.question_text: In en route adjacent-airport operations under 6-1-4b, what wake turbulence separation applies to a small aircraft behind a B757?
items.explanation: 6-1-4b3 specifies 2 minutes for a small aircraft behind a B757 in en route adjacent-airport operations.

choices.text: 5 minutes.
3 minutes.
4 minutes.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: A controller is working adjacent airports in terminal operations. An arriving Category F IFR aircraft will cross the flight path of a Category A aircraft. What separation is required under 6-1-4a?
items.explanation: 6-1-4a1 covers all Categories B through I behind Category A with 3 minutes. Category F falls within this range.

choices.text: 2 minutes because Category F is not the highest wake category.
3 minutes.
4 minutes because the larger the category gap, the larger the minimum.
No wake separation is required in terminal adjacent-airport operations.

items.question_text: Compare 6-1-4 terminal adjacent-airport wake minima with en route adjacent-airport wake minima. In terminal operations, Category B behind Category A requires 3 minutes. In en route operations, what applies to a large aircraft behind a super?
items.explanation: This tests whether the student can map terminal category-based minima to en route weight-class-based minima. 6-1-4b1 gives 3 minutes for heavy, large, or small behind super.

choices.text: No minimum applies because super aircraft are not defined in the terminal wake categories.
2 minutes because en route minima are always one minute less than terminal minima.
4 minutes because super aircraft always require one additional minute over Category A.
3 minutes (the same numeric minimum applies behind the highest wake class in each regime).

items.question_text: Per 6-1-5a, in terminal arrival operations, Category H or I aircraft behind a Category B, C, or D aircraft require _____ minutes of wake turbulence separation.
items.explanation: 6-1-5a5 specifies 3 minutes for Category H or I aircraft behind Category B, C, or D in terminal arrival operations.

items.question_text: In en route arrival operations to the same runway under 6-1-5b, a small aircraft behind a super aircraft requires _____ minutes, while a small aircraft behind a heavy aircraft requires _____ minutes.
items.explanation: 6-1-5b1(b) gives 4 minutes for small behind super (same runway); 6-1-5b2(b) gives 3 minutes for small behind heavy (same runway). The distinction is critical for same-runway en route arrivals.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-5c en route arrivals to a parallel runway separated by less than 2,500 feet (or a crossing runway with crossing projected flight paths), what separation applies to a small aircraft behind a B757?
items.explanation: 6-1-5c3 specifies 2 minutes for small behind B757 in the parallel/crossing en route case. This is different from the same-runway case in 6-1-5b3, which requires 3 minutes.

choices.text: No wake turbulence separation applies to B757 in en route arrivals.
3 minutes.
4 minutes.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: In terminal arrival operations under 6-1-5a, which of the following correctly states the separation for a Category E, F, or G aircraft behind a Category C aircraft?
items.explanation: 6-1-5a4 specifies 2 minutes for Category E, F, or G behind Category C in terminal arrivals.

choices.text: No wake turbulence separation is required for these categories.
3 minutes.
4 minutes.
2 minutes.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-5a, a Category I aircraft behind a Category E aircraft in terminal arrival operations requires 3 minutes. Under 6-1-4a4 (adjacent airport, terminal), a Category I behind Category E requires 2 minutes. What explains the difference?
items.explanation: The arrival minima in 6-1-5 apply to aircraft landing behind other arrivals to the same/parallel/crossing runways, where the wake encounter risk is highest. The adjacent-airport minima in 6-1-4 apply when flight paths merely cross at different airports, a less severe case. Knowing which paragraph to apply is operationally critical.

choices.text: The two paragraphs contradict each other and the controller may choose either minimum.
6-1-5 governs arrivals landing behind other aircraft; 6-1-4 governs crossing flight paths at adjacent airports with potentially less exposure.
6-1-4a4 applies only when both aircraft depart from the same airport, making it a departure minimum.
There is no difference; one of the paragraphs contains a typographical error.

items.question_text: In en route arrival operations under 6-1-5b (same runway), a large aircraft landing behind a super aircraft requires 3 minutes. Under 6-1-5c (parallel runway less than 2,500 ft or crossing runway), a large aircraft behind a super also requires 3 minutes. What is the operational difference between 6-1-5b and 6-1-5c?
items.explanation: 6-1-5b applies to same-runway arrivals and uses sub-categories (heavy/large vs. small) for super and heavy. 6-1-5c applies to parallel and crossing runways and uses a flatter structure. For super/heavy, the minima converge, but for B757 the parallel/crossing case (2 min) differs from the same-runway case (3 min).

choices.text: 6-1-5b applies to terminal operations; 6-1-5c applies to en route only when the runways are VFR.
There is no operational difference; the two subparagraphs are duplicates.
6-1-5b applies to departures while 6-1-5c applies to arrivals.
6-1-5b applies to same-runway arrivals; 6-1-5c applies to parallel runways separated by less than 2,500 feet or crossing runways with crossing flight paths.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-5 terminal arrival minima, which subparagraph covers the wake turbulence separation for a Category H aircraft behind a Category D aircraft and what is the correct value?
items.explanation: 6-1-5a5 explicitly covers Category H or I behind Category B, C, or D with 3 minutes. Category D is one of the lead categories in that sub-item.

choices.text: 6-1-5a6, requiring 3 minutes but that subparagraph covers only Category I behind E.
6-1-5a3, requiring 2 minutes (like Category G behind Category B).
6-1-5a5, requiring 3 minutes.
6-1-5a2, requiring 4 minutes because Category D and A are treated the same for trailing Category H/I.

items.situation: You are applying nonradar separation between two IFR aircraft using DME mileage. The pilot of one aircraft temporarily switches to a company frequency and loses direct VHF voice contact with you for approximately 90 seconds while both DME readouts continue updating.
items.question_text: During the period of lost direct voice communications, may you continue to apply mileage-based separation?

choices.text: Yes; as long as DME returns are updating, temporary loss of voice is acceptable.
No; mileage-based procedures require direct pilot/controller voice communications to be maintained.
Yes; the 90-second gap is below the standard communications-loss threshold.
Yes, but only if both aircraft have ATD as a backup to DME.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-1, mileage-based procedures and minima may be used only when direct pilot/controller VHF or UHF voice communications are maintained. Any gap in direct voice means the prerequisite is not met and the mileage-based separation becomes invalid.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-2, what is the reference point for calculating the 5-minute action deadline?

choices.text: The time the last position report was actually received.
The time the aircraft was estimated over the fix.
The time the flight plan was filed.
The time the controller first notices the missing report.

items.explanation: Per 6-1-2, the clock starts from when the aircraft was estimated over the fix, not from when the controller notices the missing report or from the last received report.

items.situation: Aircraft N123 was estimated to cross BOWIE intersection at 1415Z. At 1418Z you realize no position report has been received. N123 is at the same altitude as N456, which is 15 miles behind on the same airway and also expects to cross BOWIE.
items.question_text: What is the correct action under 6-1-2?

choices.text: Wait until 1420Z exactly because the paragraph requires waiting the full 5 minutes.
Take action to obtain the position report immediately; the report affects separation and 5 minutes from the estimate is 1420Z.
Do nothing until N456 also crosses BOWIE because separation is not yet lost.
Issue a new clearance to N456 for a different altitude and disregard the missing report.

items.explanation: 6-1-2 Requires taking action no later than 5 minutes after the estimate over the fix. Since the position report affects separation (N456 is trailing at the same altitude), action must be taken by 1420Z. The controller should not wait until the deadline to begin acting.

items.question_text: An aircraft is crossing a fix that serves as a reporting point for both an en route center and an approach control. The center has already received the position report. Under 6-1-3, may the approach control also require the pilot to report that fix?

choices.text: Yes, because the restriction applies only when both facilities are the same type.
No; the same position report must not be required by more than one facility.
Yes, because the approach control did not hear the report made to the center.
Yes, provided the report was made more than 5 minutes ago to the first facility.

items.explanation: 6-1-3 Is an absolute prohibition: do not require an aircraft to make the same position report to more than one facility. Facility type is irrelevant.

items.situation: You control adjacent airports in en route airspace. A super aircraft departs from Airport A, and 1 minute later a small IFR aircraft departs from Airport B on a course that will cross the super's flight path. Under 6-1-4b, what separation must you apply?
items.question_text: What is the required wake turbulence separation?

choices.text: 4 minutes, because small behind super always requires 4 minutes.
3 minutes, because heavy/large/small behind super requires 3 minutes.
2 minutes, because this is an en route B757 situation.
No separation is required because adjacent airports are exempt from wake turbulence rules.

items.explanation: 6-1-4B1 specifies 3 minutes for heavy, large, or small behind super in en route adjacent-airport operations. The 4-minute value applies only in the arrival context of 6-1-5, not 6-1-4.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-4a terminal adjacent-airport wake turbulence rules, which category combination is NOT one of the 2-minute minima?

choices.text: Category E, F, G, H, or I behind Category C.
Category B, C, D, E, F, G, H, or I behind Category B or D.
Category I behind Category E.
Category B, C, D, E, F, G, H, or I behind Category A.

items.explanation: 6-1-4A1 requires 3 minutes behind Category A, which is the only 3-minute minimum in terminal adjacent-airport operations. 6-1-4a2, a3, and a4 all require 2 minutes.

items.question_text: A controller recalls that 'small behind B757' always requires 3 minutes in nonradar wake turbulence. Under which 6-1 paragraphs is this recall correct or incorrect?

choices.text: Incorrect for all paragraphs; small behind B757 is always 2 minutes.
Correct in all situations because small behind B757 is always 3 minutes regardless of paragraph.
Correct only for terminal operations. En route operations do not have a B757-specific minimum.
Correct only for 6-1-5b (en route same-runway arrivals). 6-1-4b (en route adjacent) requires 2 minutes, and 6-1-5c (en route parallel/crossing) also requires 2 minutes.

items.explanation: The small-behind-B757 minimum varies: 2 minutes in 6-1-4b3 (en route adjacent airport) and 6-1-5c3 (en route parallel/crossing), but 3 minutes in 6-1-5b3 (en route same runway arrivals). No single value applies universally.

items.situation: In en route airspace, a small IFR aircraft will land behind a heavy aircraft on the same runway. Another small aircraft will land behind a heavy aircraft on a parallel runway separated by 2,000 feet.
items.question_text: What wake turbulence separation applies to each small aircraft?

choices.text: 3 minutes for both because both involve a small behind a heavy.
3 minutes for the same-runway small (6-1-5b2(b)); 2 minutes for the parallel-runway small (6-1-5c2).
2 minutes for both because parallel runways use the same minima as same runways.
4 minutes for the same-runway and 3 minutes for the parallel, because parallel runways reduce wake risk.

items.explanation: 6-1-5 Distinguishes same-runway arrivals (5b) from parallel-runway arrivals (5c). Same-runway small behind heavy = 3 minutes; parallel/crossing small behind heavy = 2 minutes.

items.situation: In terminal operations, a Category I IFR arrival will land behind a Category C arrival on the same runway. Category I is the lowest wake category and Category C is mid-range.
items.question_text: What separation must you apply under 6-1-5a?

choices.text: No wake separation is required because Category I aircraft are exempt from wake turbulence rules.
2 minutes per 6-1-5a4 (Category E, F, or G behind Category C). Category I is lower than E/F/G so a lower minimum applies.
4 minutes per 6-1-5a2 (Category H or I behind Category A). Category C is treated the same as A for the lowest trailing categories.
3 minutes per 6-1-5a5 (Category H or I behind Category B, C, or D).

items.explanation: 6-1-5A5 explicitly covers Category H or I behind Category B, C, or D, requiring 3 minutes. The controller must use the subparagraph that matches both the lead and trail categories.

items.question_text: Under 6-1-5 terminal arrival minima, which category pair has the highest (longest) required separation?

choices.text: Category B, C, D, E, F, or G behind Category A: 3 minutes.
Category H or I behind Category A: 4 minutes.
Category H or I behind Category B, C, or D: 3 minutes.
Category I behind Category E: 3 minutes.

items.explanation: 6-1-5A2 requires 4 minutes for Category H or I behind Category A, which is the longest minimum in the terminal arrival table. All other terminal arrival combinations are 3 minutes or 2 minutes.
